\section{The fitted law}
% TODO: coefficient tables + CIs for pDetA and pAssA; residual diagnostics.
Table~\ref{tab:coefficients} gives the standardised logit-GLM coefficients for detection and
association.
\IfFileExists{tables/coefficients.tex}{\input{tables/coefficients}}{}
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.86\textwidth]{figures/res_forest.pdf}
\caption[Coefficient forest plot]{Standardised GLM coefficients with group-cluster-bootstrap 95\% CIs, for
detection (\texttt{pDetA}) and association (\texttt{pAssA}). \emph{Every distance's interval crosses zero}
(grey) --- none is distinguishable from no effect.}
\label{fig:forest}
\end{figure}

\section{Variance partitioning and the intrinsic-difficulty decomposition}
A dominance (LMG/Shapley) analysis confirms the distances carry almost nothing: together the four explain only
$\approx 3\%$ of the variance in detection, no single factor dominates, and every VIF is $\approx 1$
(Table~\ref{tab:variance}) --- so the null is not an artefact of collinearity masking a real effect.
\IfFileExists{tables/variance.tex}{\input{tables/variance}}{}
Because the one apparent effect (visual
distance, below) turned out to be an animal-size confound, we pivot the hypothesis from \emph{novelty} to
intrinsic \emph{difficulty}: we promote the label-free scene properties --- low-light/IR
(\texttt{achromatic\_fraction}), clutter, and the night/IR flag, each computed per location from the frames
with no SAM\,3 run and no target label --- from nuisance covariates to predictors of interest, and test them
at the same bar. Table~\ref{tab:difficulty} decomposes the outcome. Taken \emph{with} an animal-size covariate
each difficulty signal \emph{appears} to validate (leave-species-out and leave-location-out
$\Delta\mathrm{MAE}\approx+0.017$, both significant), but a nested decomposition shows the gain is entirely
the size covariate: $\log$-area \emph{alone} carries it ($\Delta\mathrm{MAE}=+0.017$, $p=0.001/0.014$), while
low-light \emph{alone} does \emph{not} validate ($p=0.12/0.23$) and adds only $\approx+0.002$ over size. The
low-light effect is also far weaker than it first looks: the location-level night/IR--\texttt{pDetA}
correlation of $r=-0.377$ is an aggregation artefact (the ecological-correlation fallacy) --- at the cell level
it is only $-0.13$, and clutter's cell-level correlation is $0.00$. The intrinsic-difficulty pivot is
therefore \textbf{also null}: the only label-free property that predicts detection is animal size.
\IfFileExists{tables/difficulty.tex}{\input{tables/difficulty}}{}

\section{Out-of-sample validation}
% TODO: predictive-line figures.
Table~\ref{tab:cv} reports whole-group hold-out error against a mean-predictor baseline. This is the headline
result and it is a \textbf{null}: on both the location and the species schemes, and for both targets, no
distance model beats simply predicting the mean --- every $\Delta\mathrm{MAE}$ is within noise of zero and no
gain is significant under the paired group-bootstrap. \textbf{This is not an underpowered test.} The same GLM
and grouped cross-validation, on the same cells, \emph{do} detect a real signal when one exists: animal size
($\log$-area) validates out of sample at the identical bar ($\Delta\mathrm{MAE}=+0.017$, $p<0.017$ on both
schemes; Table~\ref{tab:difficulty}). A test that fires for size yet stays flat for the distances is evidence
of a genuine \emph{absence} of before-running label-free signal, not of low power.
\IfFileExists{tables/cv_validation.tex}{\input{tables/cv_validation}}{}
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.52\textwidth]{figures/res_pred_vs_actual.pdf}
\caption[Out-of-sample predicted vs actual]{Out-of-sample predictions vs the truth for detection
(\texttt{pDetA}), on held-out species and locations. The predictions collapse onto the mean baseline (orange)
instead of tracking the diagonal (dashed) --- the distances carry no out-of-sample predictive power.}
\label{fig:predvsactual}
\end{figure}

\section{Species hold-out (H1): novelty and the size confound}
The primary novelty test --- leave-one-species-out over the pooled train+test species (99 species,
1025 scored cells) --- is in Tables~\ref{tab:gate1_species}--\ref{tab:cv_species}. Out-of-sample
prediction is not significant on any hold-out (Table~\ref{tab:cv_species}). The only coefficient whose CI
excludes zero, \emph{visual distance} (Table~\ref{tab:coefficients_species}), is positive --- the wrong
sign for H1 --- and is a \textbf{confound}: visually-distinctive species are larger
(visual~$\leftrightarrow$~$\log$-area $r=0.22$) and larger animals score higher
($\log$-area~$\leftrightarrow$~pDetA $r=0.30$, the strongest raw correlate of the score). Adding an animal-size
covariate shrinks the visual coefficient from $+0.37$ to $+0.22$ (CI now spanning zero) while size itself
is the significant driver, so ``visual novelty helps'' was really ``bigger animals are easier''. Taxonomic
novelty is flat ($r\approx0.00$). Novelty does not predict transfer.
\IfFileExists{tables/gate1_measurement_species.tex}{\input{tables/gate1_measurement_species}}{}
\IfFileExists{tables/coefficients_species.tex}{\input{tables/coefficients_species}}{}
\IfFileExists{tables/cv_validation_species.tex}{\input{tables/cv_validation_species}}{}
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.52\textwidth]{figures/res_size_confound.pdf}
\caption[The size confound]{The one label-free property that predicts detection: animal size. Per-cell
\texttt{pDetA} rises with the log ground-truth mask area ($r=+0.31$) --- the confound behind the apparent
visual-distance effect.}
\label{fig:size}
\end{figure}

\section{False positives / hallucination}
The detection score conditions on the animal being present, so it measures recall, not hallucination. The
$1486$ \emph{hard negatives} (queries for absent species) ask the complementary question, and are the
closest the study comes to a validated before-running signal. SAM\,3 returns a masklet anyway about
$\mathbf{9.8\%}$ of the time. This hallucination rate does \emph{not} rise with taxonomic novelty
($r\approx+0.06$, n.s.); it \emph{falls} with visual distinctiveness ($r\approx-0.33$), and this survives an
animal-size control (partial $-0.37$) --- a genuine, size-independent, label-free correlate, though its
direction (``distinctive $=$ safer'') is the \emph{opposite} of ``novelty hurts''. Crucially it is a
\textbf{correlate, not a predictor}: predicting a held-out species' hallucination rate from visual distance
beats a mean baseline by only $+0.008$~MAE and \emph{just misses} out-of-sample significance ($p=0.053$;
Table~\ref{tab:fp}). Even the study's best signal does not clear the bar.
\IfFileExists{tables/false_positives.tex}{\input{tables/false_positives}}{}

\section{Ablations and robustness}
\paragraph{Factor ablation.} Refitting detection with each distance dropped in turn barely moves the fit or
the (null) out-of-sample gain (Table~\ref{tab:ablation}): no single distance carries signal. The only design
that changes anything is adding the animal-size covariate, which alone lifts the out-of-sample gain to
significance ($\Delta\mathrm{MAE}\approx+0.02$, $p\!\le\!0.02$) --- again consistent with size being the sole
real correlate. Removing the support covariate does not rescue a distance effect either. The variance
partition (Table~\ref{tab:variance}) tells the same story from the other direction.
\IfFileExists{tables/ablations.tex}{\input{tables/ablations}}{}

\paragraph{Design robustness (future work).} Three further checks --- a CLIP rather than DINOv2 visual
encoder, whole-frame rather than mask-cropped visual embeddings, and a generic ``animal'' rather than a
species-specific prompt --- each require a fresh GPU inference or embedding pass and are left for future work.
Given that the null is flat across the factor ablation, the variance partition, both hold-out schemes, and the
intrinsic-difficulty pivot, a change of encoder, crop, or prompt is unlikely to overturn it.
